Name: training-free_spectral_quantization
Title: Spectral Sensitivity Analysis for Training-Free Mixed Precision Quantization
Short Hypothesis: The spectral properties of layer-wise activations and weights can serve as a deterministic proxy for mixed precision quantization, enabling training-free bit-width allocation with minimal computational overhead.
Related Work: Existing methods like EMQ and InfoQ rely on heuristics or evolutionary strategies for bit-width allocation, often requiring extensive calibration or training. Our approach leverages spectral decomposition to directly quantify layer sensitivity without training, distinguishing it by its mathematical transparency and computational efficiency.
Abstract: Mixed precision quantization (MPQ) offers a promising path to efficient neural network inference, but existing methods often rely on heuristic or training-intensive approaches for bit-width allocation. We propose a novel, training-free framework that leverages spectral sensitivity analysis to determine optimal bit-width configurations. By decomposing layer-wise activations and weights into their spectral components, we quantify sensitivity through condition numbers and singular value distributions. This approach enables deterministic bit-width allocation with minimal computational overhead, ensuring both accuracy and efficiency. Our experiments on ResNet and MobileNet architectures demonstrate that spectral sensitivity analysis outperforms existing training-free proxies, achieving superior accuracy-complexity trade-offs.
Experiments: Conduct spectral decomposition on layer-wise activations and weights of ResNet-18 and MobileNetV2 using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).
Quantify sensitivity metrics (e.g., condition number, singular value decay rate) for each layer.
Formulate bit-width allocation as an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) problem, minimizing total sensitivity under a given resource budget.
Evaluate performance on ImageNet, comparing accuracy and inference speed against EMQ and InfoQ.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of spectral metrics on bit-width allocation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Spectral decomposition may introduce computational overhead for very large models.
The effectiveness of spectral metrics may vary across architectures or tasks.
The proposed method assumes layer-wise independence, potentially overlooking inter-layer dependencies.
